behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): this is a handoff behavior between HTTP request parsing/raw-body extraction, signature verification, and queue forwarding; a unit test of HMAC logic alone can miss forwarding-path bugs. Rationale B (infra check): no real OS/network/subprocess is required; an in-process app + injected queue spy is enough. Rejection gate: System rejected because behavior text only requires 'rejected with 401' and 'not forwarded', not real network conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Start the handler with real request parsing/signature middleware wired to a fake queue module. Send a signed request where body bytes are altered after signature creation. Assert HTTP 401 and assert queue write was never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): ordering bug appears across handler->queue persistence boundary, not inside one pure function. Rationale B (infra check): can be proven with local persistent store (e.g., sqlite/file-backed queue) in-process; no external OS/network primitive beyond local module wiring is needed. Rejection gate: System rejected because behavior text is about ordering of local enqueue vs response, not real HTTP/network failure modes.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler to an actual local persistent queue implementation (test DB/file). Instrument queue write completion and response send timestamps/events. Assert durable enqueue completion occurs before HTTP 200 is emitted.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): this validates decoupling between request path and downstream worker path (module interaction), not a single function branch. Rationale B (infra check): a delayed fake downstream processor in-process reproduces the failure; no real OS/network/subprocess needed. Rejection gate: System rejected because behavior text only requires non-blocking relative to downstream completion, not real network/load effects.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler with queue + downstream worker module where processing promise is intentionally delayed. Post an event and assert response is returned before worker promise resolves.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): wall-clock latency under concurrency depends on event loop scheduling, real server behavior, and queue/worker contention. Rationale B (infra check): mocks/fake timers can hide starvation/backpressure issues; this needs real timing and concurrent request execution. Rejection gate: Integration rejected because behavior text explicitly requires 'within 3 seconds wall-clock time' under 'slow and concurrent' conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Launch the service process with real HTTP listener and queue backend used in deployment-like mode. Generate concurrent load with earlier events triggering slow downstream work; measure end-to-end response latency for new requests and assert p100 (or strict per-request) <= 3s.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): idempotency failure appears across request handling + dedupe state + queue write interaction, especially across separate requests. Rationale B (infra check): can be reproduced with in-process modules and local persistence; no real network/OS primitive required. Rejection gate: System rejected because behavior text does not require distributed/network race semantics, only duplicate-ID suppression.
behaviors.test_description: Send two requests with same event ID through the wired handler+dedupe+queue stack. Assert first request enqueues once and second request does not create another queue record.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): this validates mapping of queue-layer capacity signal to HTTP contract (status + header), a cross-module handoff behavior. Rationale B (infra check): a queue module that deterministically returns 'full' is sufficient; no real OS/network/subprocess required. Rejection gate: System rejected because behavior text is about response mapping when 'queue reports it is full', not testing real infrastructure saturation.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler to queue interface implementation that returns a full-capacity error. Send a request and assert HTTP 503 and presence/value format of Retry-After header.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): this is queue component behavior across config->storage/write path, not just one pure function. Rationale B (infra check): requires real local persistence adapter used by queue (DB/file) to catch enforcement bugs; still does not require subprocess/external network. Rejection gate: System rejected because behavior text only asserts capacity enforcement in the persistent queue itself, with no real HTTP/OS event dependency beyond local storage integration.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue backend with small capacity (e.g., 2). Enqueue up to capacity, then enqueue one more and assert rejection/error type plus no extra persisted record.
